0.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §3.1 General Requirements for Gas Detection]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 3.1 — General Requirements for Gas Detection

All petroleum processing installations, refineries, petrochemical complexes, and associated hazardous area facilities shall install and maintain a comprehensive gas detection system in accordance with the provisions of this standard. The gas detection system shall be designed to detect toxic, flammable, and asphyxiating gases in concentrations that may pose a risk to personnel or plant integrity.

3.1.1 Every installation shall identify all potential gas release points including flanged joints, valve packings, pump seals, compressor seals, heat exchanger tube sheets, and any other potential leak sources. A formal gas hazard assessment shall be conducted by a qualified engineer prior to commissioning and reviewed at minimum every three years.

3.1.2 Fixed gas detectors shall be installed at strategic locations based on the gas hazard assessment. Portable gas detectors shall be available and issued to all personnel entering hazardous areas for operational tasks.

3.1.3 Gas detection systems shall be capable of detecting: (a) flammable gases at 0-100% Lower Explosive Limit (LEL), with alarms at 10% LEL and 25% LEL; (b) oxygen deficiency below 19.5% v/v and oxygen enrichment above 23.5% v/v; (c) hydrogen sulfide (H2S) at concentrations from 1 ppm to at least 100 ppm with IDLH alarm at 10 ppm; (d) carbon monoxide (CO) at concentrations from 10 ppm to 500 ppm.

3.1.4 All gas detection instrumentation shall be tested, calibrated, and certified by competent persons. Calibration records shall be maintained for a minimum period of five years and be available for inspection by statutory authorities.

3.1.5 Alarm setpoints shall be established based on occupational exposure limits, IDLH values, and process safety considerations. All alarms shall be distinctly audible above ambient noise levels and supplemented by visual alarms in high-noise environments.

2.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §4.2 Fixed Gas Detection Systems]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 4.2 — Fixed Gas Detection Systems

Fixed gas detection systems form the primary line of defense in continuous monitoring of hazardous areas. All fixed systems shall be designed in accordance with IEC 60079-29 and shall meet the requirements of this section.

4.2.1 DETECTOR PLACEMENT: Fixed detectors shall be placed at heights appropriate to the density of the target gas relative to air. For gases heavier than air (e.g., H2S, propane, butane), detectors shall be positioned 300-500 mm above floor level or at the lowest point of potential accumulation. For gases lighter than air (e.g., hydrogen, methane), detectors shall be positioned near the ceiling or at least 200 mm from the ceiling to avoid dead zones.

4.2.2 COVERAGE REQUIREMENTS: In coke oven battery areas, sour crude processing units, and sulphur recovery units where H2S is a primary hazard, fixed H2S detectors shall be placed at maximum 10-metre spacing. In open process areas, detector spacing shall not exceed 15 metres. In enclosed or semi-enclosed structures, spacing shall not exceed 8 metres.

4.2.3 SYSTEM INTEGRITY: Fixed gas detection systems shall be connected to a continuously monitored control panel. The system shall have a fail-safe design such that loss of power or signal from any detector triggers an alert at the central monitoring station. Systems shall have battery backup capable of sustaining at least four hours of operation following mains power failure.

4.2.4 INTEGRATION WITH PERMIT SYSTEM: Fixed gas detector readings shall be electronically accessible from the permit-to-work control station. Before issuing any hot work permit in a hazardous area, the permit authority shall verify current gas readings from all fixed detectors within a 25-metre radius of the proposed work location.

4.2.5 MAINTENANCE FREQUENCY: Fixed detectors shall be functionally tested monthly and calibrated quarterly using certified calibration gases. Annual third-party verification of the entire fixed gas detection system shall be conducted by an accredited testing laboratory.

3.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §4.2.3 Response to Gas Alarms - Priority Actions]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 4.2.3 — Response to Gas Alarms: Priority Actions

When a gas alarm activates, immediate and coordinated response is mandatory. The following protocol defines the priority response actions for all gas alarm levels.

LEVEL-1 ALARM (LOW-LOW / ADVISORY): H2S 1-9 ppm, LEL 10-24%, CO 25-49 ppm, O2 19.5-20.5% (deficiency trending)
Action: (a) Investigate source immediately; (b) notify area supervisor; (c) increase monitoring frequency to continuous; (d) review all active permits in the affected zone; (e) confirm all personnel are wearing appropriate PPE.

LEVEL-2 ALARM (LOW / CAUTION): H2S 10-34 ppm, LEL 25-49%, CO 50-199 ppm, O2 below 19.5% or above 23.5%
Action: (a) SUSPEND all hot work in the affected zone IMMEDIATELY — no exceptions; (b) evacuate non-essential personnel from a 50-metre radius; (c) activate emergency response team; (d) initiate isolation of gas source if safely possible; (e) don SCBA for all personnel remaining in zone; (f) notify site emergency coordinator.

LEVEL-3 ALARM (HIGH / EMERGENCY): H2S 35 ppm and above, LEL 50% and above, CO 200 ppm and above
Action: (a) IMMEDIATE TOTAL EVACUATION of all personnel from a 150-metre radius; (b) activate site emergency alarm; (c) suspend ALL operations in the affected area; (d) isolate all ignition sources; (e) notify fire brigade and emergency services; (f) do NOT re-enter without authorization from incident commander using SCBA; (g) account for all personnel at muster points.

CRITICAL NOTE: An H2S reading of 35 ppm or above constitutes an immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH) condition. Any personnel exposed without SCBA must receive immediate medical evaluation. All active permits in the affected zone are automatically CANCELLED upon Level-3 alarm activation and must be re-issued from scratch following full investigation and area clearance.

4.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §5.2 Simultaneous Operations (SIMOPS) Management]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 5.2 — Simultaneous Operations (SIMOPS) Management

Simultaneous operations occur when two or more work activities are conducted in the same or adjacent hazardous areas concurrently. SIMOPS situations demand elevated levels of coordination, communication, and gas monitoring to prevent compound hazard scenarios.

5.2.1 SIMOPS IDENTIFICATION: A SIMOPS situation exists whenever: (a) hot work and confined space entry occur within 30 metres of each other; (b) two or more permits are active in the same process unit or battery; (c) maintenance activities occur while production operations are ongoing in the same zone; (d) vehicle operations occur in areas with active hot work permits.

5.2.2 SIMOPS APPROVAL AUTHORITY: All SIMOPS scenarios shall be identified during the permit-to-work planning stage. A dedicated SIMOPS risk assessment shall be conducted and approved by the Operations Manager (or equivalent senior authority) before any SIMOPS activity commences. No individual supervisor can approve SIMOPS without documented risk assessment.

5.2.3 GAS MONITORING DURING SIMOPS: During any SIMOPS involving hot work, gas monitoring shall be continuous and not intermittent. At minimum, one dedicated gas monitor (fixed or portable) shall be assigned to each work team. If H2S is a credible hazard, each person in the area shall carry an individual H2S personal alarm with audible and vibration alert.

5.2.4 COMMUNICATION PROTOCOL: A SIMOPS coordinator shall be designated with authority to halt any or all activities if a conflict or hazard emerges. All work teams shall maintain radio contact with the SIMOPS coordinator at intervals not exceeding 15 minutes. A SIMOPS board shall be maintained at the site entrance showing all active permits and their locations.

5.2.5 EXCLUSION ZONES: During hot work, an exclusion zone of minimum 15 metres radius from the work point shall be maintained free of other permit-to-work activities. This exclusion distance shall be increased to 30 metres if H2S concentration anywhere in the area has exceeded 5 ppm in the preceding 2 hours.

5.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §5.2.1 Permit Conflicts in Same Zone]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 5.2.1 — Permit Conflicts in Same Zone

Permit conflicts arise when two or more work activities with incompatible hazard profiles are authorized to proceed simultaneously in the same zone. This section provides mandatory guidance for identifying, preventing, and resolving permit conflicts.

CONFLICT CATEGORIES:
Category A — ABSOLUTE CONFLICT (activities that can never be simultaneously permitted):
(i) Hot work and confined space entry with active gas risk in the same zone;
(ii) Hot work and flammable liquid transfer within 30 metres;
(iii) Hot work and H2S-generating maintenance activities in the same process unit.

Category B — CONDITIONAL CONFLICT (activities requiring enhanced controls to proceed simultaneously):
(i) Two confined space entries in connected vessels or adjacent structures;
(ii) Hot work and cold work in adjacent zones separated by less than 15 metres;
(iii) Any permit activity and emergency isolation/depressurization operations.

RESOLUTION PROCEDURE: When a permit conflict is identified: (a) the later-issued permit shall be held pending conflict review; (b) the Permit Authority shall convene a joint safety review with all permit holders; (c) a SIMOPS assessment shall be conducted; (d) only Category B conflicts may be resolved with enhanced controls — Category A conflicts must be sequenced, not simultaneous; (e) all conflict resolution decisions shall be documented in the permit register.

CRITICAL RULE: If fixed or portable gas detectors in the zone show H2S at or above 10 ppm, ALL Category B conflicts automatically elevate to Category A. No simultaneous operations shall be permitted. The area shall be treated as an active H2S emergency and the Level-2 alarm response protocol from Section 4.2.3 shall be initiated immediately.

7.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §6.3.2 Hot Work Near Toxic Gas Sources]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 6.3.2 — Hot Work Near Toxic Gas Sources

When hot work is planned in areas where toxic gas sources (H2S, CO, NH3, SO2, HCN, or other Schedule 1 toxic substances) are present, additional mandatory controls apply over and above the standard hot work procedure.

TOXIC GAS SOURCE IDENTIFICATION: Prior to issuing a hot work permit near toxic gas sources, the Permit Authority shall: (a) identify all toxic gas sources within a 30-metre radius; (b) review the gas readings log for the previous 24 hours at all fixed detectors in the area; (c) consult with the process/operations team to determine if any H2S-generating process upsets are expected; (d) confirm the prevailing wind direction and ensure downwind evacuation routes are identified.

H2S-SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS: In coke oven battery areas, sour crude processing units, amine treating units, sour water strippers, and sulphur recovery units — areas classified as H2S-endemic — the following additional requirements apply for hot work:

(i) MANDATORY: If any fixed detector in the area has recorded H2S above 10 ppm in the 24 hours preceding the planned hot work, a formal H2S Risk Review must be completed before the permit can be issued. This review requires sign-off from the Safety Officer in addition to the normal Permit Authority.

(ii) All hot work personnel in H2S-endemic areas must be equipped with: personal H2S detector (with alarm), and SCBA on standby within 15 metres of the work site. SCBA must be in ready-to-don condition.

(iii) A dedicated gas monitor must continuously monitor H2S at the work site. If H2S exceeds 5 ppm, work stops. If H2S exceeds 10 ppm, immediate evacuation.

(iv) Hot work duration in H2S-endemic areas is limited to 4-hour work cycles with mandatory 30-minute breaks for personnel rotation. No worker shall accumulate more than 8 hours of H2S-area exposure in a 24-hour period.

8.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-2 §2.1 Pressure Monitoring Requirements]
OISD Standard GS-2: Blow Down and Pressure Relief Systems
Section 2.1 — Pressure Monitoring Requirements

Pressure monitoring is integral to the safe operation of all pressurized vessels, pipelines, and process systems in petroleum and petrochemical facilities. Anomalous pressure conditions — whether over-pressure or rapid pressure loss — are frequently precursors to catastrophic failure events.

2.1.1 MANDATORY INSTRUMENTATION: All pressure vessels operating above 1.5 kg/cm² gauge pressure shall be equipped with: (a) at least two independent pressure gauges installed in separate tapping points; (b) a pressure transmitter connected to the plant Distributed Control System (DCS) with alarm capability; (c) a pressure relief valve (PRV) sized for the maximum credible over-pressure scenario.

2.1.2 ALARM SETPOINTS FOR PRESSURE: Pressure alarms shall be set at: High-High (HH) = 90% of design pressure, triggering automatic ESD (Emergency Shutdown) initiation; High (H) = 80% of design pressure, requiring operator investigation; Low (L) = 10% above minimum operating pressure, indicating potential leak or process upset; Low-Low (LL) = minimum safe operating pressure, triggering ESD or manual isolation.

2.1.3 PRESSURE MONITORING IN COKE OVEN GAS SYSTEMS: Coke oven gas (COG) systems, which contain significant concentrations of H2S (typically 500-2000 mg/Nm³), CO (5-8% v/v), and flammable hydrocarbons (LEL ~4%), require special pressure monitoring protocols. Coke oven battery collecting mains shall maintain slight positive pressure (2-10 mm water column) to prevent air ingress. Loss of positive pressure shall trigger immediate investigation as it may indicate a breach that risks H2S and CO exposure to personnel.

2.1.4 DOCUMENTATION: All pressure readings for critical process systems shall be logged at minimum every two hours. DCS trend data shall be retained for minimum 90 days. Anomalous readings shall be noted in the shift log and communicated at handover.

9.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-2 §2.4 Pressure Rise Response Protocol]
OISD Standard GS-2: Blow Down and Pressure Relief Systems
Section 2.4 — Pressure Rise Response Protocol

Rapid or unexpected pressure rise in process vessels is a potential precursor to catastrophic failure, fire, or explosion. This section prescribes the mandatory response protocol for all pressure rise events.

2.4.1 IMMEDIATE RESPONSE: Upon detection of pressure rising towards the High (H) alarm setpoint: (a) operator shall immediately investigate the cause — typical causes include blocked outlet, runaway reaction, failed control valve, or loss of cooling; (b) operator shall attempt manual pressure reduction through established safe procedures; (c) if pressure continues to rise and reaches High-High (HH) setpoint, the Emergency Shutdown System (ESD) shall be activated immediately, whether automatically or manually.

2.4.2 BLOW DOWN INITIATION: If ESD initiation does not arrest pressure rise, or if ESD fails, the Blow Down system shall be activated to safely depressurize the vessel to the flare system. Blow down shall proceed only if: (a) the flare system is confirmed operational; (b) cross-contamination risks with other systems connected to the flare header are assessed; (c) flare tip ignition is confirmed before major blow down flow.

2.4.3 EVACUATION DURING PRESSURE EMERGENCY: During any vessel over-pressure event, all non-essential personnel shall be evacuated from a radius of: 50 metres for vessels < 10 m³; 100 metres for vessels 10-100 m³; 200 metres for vessels > 100 m³. If H2S is a component of the vessel contents, these evacuation distances shall be increased by 50%.

2.4.4 DOCUMENTATION AND INVESTIGATION: Every pressure relief event (PRV lift, ESD activation, or blow down) shall be treated as a Process Safety Event. A formal investigation shall be completed within 48 hours, with root cause analysis within 7 days. All such events shall be reported to the corporate safety function and to statutory authorities per the applicable regulations.

10.text:
[SOURCE: FACTORY ACT 1948 Section 41B - Compulsory Disclosure of Information by Occupier]
THE FACTORIES ACT 1948 (as amended)
Chapter IV-A: Special Provisions Relating to Hazardous Processes
Section 41B — Compulsory Disclosure of Information by Occupier

The occupier of every factory carrying on a hazardous process shall disclose, in the manner prescribed, to the workers employed therein and to the Chief Inspector all information regarding the dangers, including health hazards and the measures to overcome such hazards, arising from exposure to or handling of materials or substances in the manufacture of articles in such factory.

41B(1) Every occupier of a factory engaged in a hazardous process shall: (a) maintain accurate and up-to-date health records for each worker employed in the hazardous process and such records shall be accessible to the workers concerned; (b) appoint a Safety Officer possessing such qualifications and experience as may be prescribed; (c) provide for medical examination of every worker: (i) before such worker is assigned to a hazardous process, and (ii) while such worker is engaged in a hazardous process, and (iii) after such worker has ceased to be so engaged.

41B(2) The occupier shall ensure that all workers employed in hazardous areas are informed of: (a) the nature of the hazardous substances they may be exposed to; (b) the health risks associated with such exposure; (c) the emergency procedures to be followed in the event of an accidental release; (d) the location and proper use of all safety equipment, including gas detectors, SCBA, and first aid facilities.

RELEVANCE TO GAS HAZARDS: For facilities handling H2S, CO, or other toxic gases, Section 41B requires that: workers are briefed on IDLH concentrations and symptoms; emergency response procedures including evacuation routes are communicated and drilled; gas monitoring data is made accessible to workers; and incident data is not concealed from workers or inspectors.

11.text:
[SOURCE: FACTORY ACT 1948 Section 41G - Workers Participation in Safety Management]
THE FACTORIES ACT 1948 (as amended)
Chapter IV-A: Special Provisions Relating to Hazardous Processes
Section 41G — Workers Participation in Safety Management

41G(1) The occupier of every factory carrying on any hazardous process shall, in consultation with the Chief Inspector, set up a Safety Committee consisting of equal number of representatives of workers and management.

41G(2) The Safety Committee shall meet at least once in every quarter. The minutes of such meetings shall be recorded and maintained for inspection.

41G(3) The Safety Committee shall have the following functions: (a) to identify possible causes of accidents and suggest remedial measures; (b) to evaluate effectiveness of safety training; (c) to review investigation reports of accidents, dangerous occurrences, and occupational diseases; (d) to recommend improvements in work procedures where safety hazards exist; (e) to review the implementation of safety recommendations from previous meetings.

41G(4) Workers' representatives on the Safety Committee shall have the right to: (a) inspect any part of the factory at any time for the purpose of identifying hazards; (b) access accident investigation reports and gas monitoring logs; (c) report hazardous conditions to the management without fear of victimization; (d) participate in any inspection or survey conducted by an Inspector under this Act.

41G(5) If the Safety Committee determines that a working condition constitutes an imminent serious danger to the life or health of workers, the Committee may direct suspension of the hazardous activity and refer the matter to the Chief Inspector. No worker shall be penalized for raising safety concerns through the Safety Committee.

PRACTICAL NOTE: Safety Committees in facilities with H2S hazards should include periodic review of gas monitoring trends, hotwork permit statistics, and SIMOPS incident reports as standing agenda items.

12.text:
[SOURCE: FACTORY ACT 1948 Rule 68N - Permit to Work System Requirements]
THE FACTORIES ACT 1948 (as amended) — RULES
Rule 68N — Permit to Work System Requirements for Hazardous Processes

68N(1) APPLICABILITY: The Permit to Work (PTW) system requirements of this rule apply to all factories carrying on hazardous processes as defined under Schedule 1 of the Factories Act 1948. This includes petroleum refineries, petrochemical plants, coke oven plants, chemical manufacturing facilities, and any other facility handling Schedule 1 hazardous substances.

68N(2) MANDATORY ELEMENTS OF A PTW: Every work permit issued for work in a hazardous area shall contain: (a) unique permit identification number; (b) exact location of the work, including equipment tag number and plant area designation; (c) description of the work to be performed; (d) hazard identification including all gases, chemicals, and energy sources present; (e) gas test results signed by the gas tester, including time, location, and readings for all relevant gases; (f) isolation register reference, confirming all energy sources have been isolated; (g) PPE requirements specific to the identified hazards; (h) emergency procedures and nearest SCBA location; (i) validity period (start time and expiry time); (j) signatures of the Performing Authority (person doing the work) and the Area/Issuing Authority (person authorizing the work).

68N(3) GAS TESTING REQUIREMENTS: Gas testing shall be conducted by a competent person using a calibrated multi-gas detector. Testing shall occur: (a) immediately before issuing the permit; (b) at intervals not exceeding 30 minutes during work that carries a gas risk; (c) after any work interruption of 15 minutes or more before work resumes; (d) whenever any change in wind direction, process conditions, or nearby operations occurs.

68N(4) CANCELLATION: A permit is automatically cancelled upon: (a) gas alarm at any level in the work area; (b) change in work scope; (c) change in area conditions; (d) shift change (permits are shift-specific and cannot carry over).

13.text:
[SOURCE: DGMS Circular 2019-07 - Combined Gas Monitoring Requirements]
DIRECTORATE GENERAL OF MINES SAFETY (DGMS)
Circular No. 2019-07
Subject: Combined Gas Monitoring Requirements in Coke Oven and By-Product Recovery Plants

To: All Owners, Agents, and Managers of Coke Oven Plants operating under the Mines Act 1952

Following a review of incidents in coke oven facilities over the 2015-2019 period, this Circular mandates enhanced combined gas monitoring requirements for all coke oven battery areas and associated by-product recovery plants.

BACKGROUND: Analysis of incident data reveals that isolated gas readings (single-gas monitoring) frequently fail to capture the full hazard picture in coke oven environments. Coke oven gas (COG) is a complex mixture containing H2S (typically 500-2000 mg/Nm³), CO (5-8% v/v), benzene, naphthalene, ammonia, and a mixture of hydrocarbons (combined LEL of approximately 4%). Events that appeared as single-gas incidents were frequently found, on post-incident investigation, to involve simultaneous exposure to multiple toxic and flammable components.

MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS EFFECTIVE IMMEDIATELY:

(a) MULTI-GAS MONITORING: All personnel entering coke oven battery areas, gas cleaning plants, and tar decanter areas shall carry a minimum 4-gas detector capable of simultaneously reading O2, LEL, H2S, and CO.

(b) COMBINED RISK THRESHOLD: Even where individual gas readings are below alarm setpoints, if H2S is in range 10-20 ppm AND CO is in range 25-75 ppm simultaneously, this combined reading shall be treated as a Level-2 (Caution) alarm condition, regardless of individual alarm thresholds. The combined toxic burden presents a risk disproportionate to individual readings.

(c) LOGGING: All multi-gas detector readings taken during work in coke oven areas shall be logged electronically at minimum every 30 minutes, with location tagging. Paper logs are no longer acceptable as primary records.

(d) CROSS-REFERENCING WITH PTW: Before issuing any PTW in coke oven areas, the Permit Authority shall consult the gas monitoring log for the previous 2 hours at the intended work location.

14.text:
[SOURCE: DGMS Circular 2021-03 - Coke Oven Battery Safety Protocols Post-Incidents]
DIRECTORATE GENERAL OF MINES SAFETY (DGMS)
Circular No. 2021-03
Subject: Enhanced Safety Protocols for Coke Oven Battery Operations Following Fatality Incidents

To: All Owners, Agents, and Managers of Integrated Steel Plants with Coke Oven Batteries

BACKGROUND: Between January 2019 and December 2020, five fatal incidents occurred in coke oven battery operations across three major steel plants in India. DGMS investigation teams completed detailed root cause analyses of all five incidents. This Circular presents the common causation factors and mandates corrective protocols.

COMMON CAUSATION FACTORS ACROSS ALL FIVE INCIDENTS:

1. PERMIT SYSTEM FAILURE: In all five cases, a hot work permit was active in the zone at the time of the fatality. In three of the five cases, the hot work permit was issued without cross-referencing gas monitoring logs that showed H2S readings above 10 ppm in the preceding hours.

2. CONFINED SPACE CONCURRENT WITH HOT WORK: In four of five cases, confined space entry was occurring concurrently with nearby hot work. No SIMOPS assessment had been completed. The joint hazard — hot work ignition risk combined with toxic gas accumulation in the confined space — was not evaluated.

3. INADEQUATE SCBA AVAILABILITY: In all five cases, SCBA sets were available on site but were not within the specified standby distance of 15 metres from the work point.

4. SHIFT HANDOVER GAP: In three of five cases, the incident occurred within 90 minutes of a shift handover. Investigation found that the elevated gas readings from the outgoing shift were not communicated to the incoming shift or were minimized as "routine."

MANDATORY CORRECTIVE PROTOCOLS:

(A) Triple-check protocol: Any PTW in coke oven areas must include three independent gas tests — initial, re-test 30 minutes later, and confirmation test immediately before work starts. All three must confirm safe conditions.

(B) SIMOPS mandatory assessment for any concurrent confined space + hot work within 30 metres in coke oven areas.

(C) SCBA ready-to-use within 10 metres (not 15 metres) for all hot work in coke oven battery zones.

(D) Shift handover gas briefing: Incoming shift supervisor must sign a dedicated "Gas Status Received" form before assuming charge.

15.text:
[SOURCE: VIZAG INCIDENT ANALYSIS - H2S + Hot Work + Confined Space Pattern]
VIZAG STEEL PLANT SAFETY ANALYSIS — SYNTHESIZED INCIDENT PATTERN REVIEW
Document Type: Internal Safety Intelligence Report (Synthesized from Investigations)
Reference: Pattern Analysis: H2S 35-50 ppm + Active Hot Work Permit + Confined Space Entry

INCIDENT PATTERN IDENTIFIED: Analysis of five fatal incidents at coke oven battery and associated facilities reveals a consistent and recurring causal pattern:

PRIMARY PATTERN: H2S reading 35-50 ppm (at or near IDLH) + Active Hot Work Permit in adjacent area + Simultaneous Confined Space Entry = CRITICAL RISK / HIGH PROBABILITY OF FATALITY

INCIDENT DETAIL SUMMARIES:

Incident V-1: H2S reading 41 ppm recorded at Battery #4 charging floor. Active hot work permit for flange replacement was in progress 18 metres away. A worker entered a collecting main inspection manhole (confined space) without verifying current H2S levels. H2S in the confined space was subsequently measured at 72 ppm. Worker became incapacitated within 2 minutes. Fatality resulted.

Incident V-2: H2S readings 35-38 ppm recorded over 90-minute period on outgoing shift. Shift handover occurred without formal gas briefing. Incoming shift issued a hot work permit for oven door seal welding. Worker in adjacent confined sump for cleaning simultaneously. H2S spike to 55 ppm during welding (heat-aggravated off-gassing). One fatality, one serious injury.

Incident V-3: H2S at 47 ppm, CO at 180 ppm (combined toxic burden). Hot work permit active. Confined space entry for pump maintenance. No SCBA at work site (stored 40 metres away). Two fatalities.

ROOT CAUSE COMMON TO ALL FIVE INCIDENTS: Absence of cross-referencing between real-time gas monitoring readings and the active permit-to-work system. Gas readings above 35 ppm H2S were visible in the monitoring system but no mechanism existed to automatically flag or prevent issuance of concurrent hot work or confined space entry permits.

CRITICAL THRESHOLD IDENTIFIED: H2S 35 ppm is the site-specific trigger level where risk of fatality becomes acute if hot work or confined space operations are concurrent.

16.text:
[SOURCE: VIZAG INCIDENT ANALYSIS - Root Cause: No Cross-Reference Between Gas Readings and Permit System]
VIZAG STEEL PLANT SAFETY ANALYSIS — ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS
Pattern: Failure of Integration Between Gas Monitoring and Permit-to-Work System

DETAILED ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:

ROOT CAUSE 1 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE FAILURE: The gas monitoring system and the permit-to-work system operated as completely independent systems with no data integration. Permit-issuing supervisors had no automated alert or mandatory check requiring review of gas monitoring data before issuing permits. The two systems were on different software platforms with no interoperability.

ROOT CAUSE 2 — PROCEDURAL GAP: Procedure documents required gas testing at the time of permit issue, but did not require review of historical gas data from fixed monitors. A one-time gas test at the moment of permit issue could pass (e.g., momentary favorable wind direction) while historical data showed a developing H2S release trend.

ROOT CAUSE 3 — SHIFT HANDOVER FRAGMENTATION: The shift handover process treated gas monitoring and permit management as separate items in the handover checklist. There was no single consolidated "Area Hazard Status" document that linked elevated gas readings with active and planned permits.

ROOT CAUSE 4 — SUPERVISOR OVERLOAD: Shift supervisors were responsible for both production targets and safety management. In three of five incidents, the supervisors who issued the permits were under production pressure to complete the maintenance work before the end of shift.

RECOMMENDED CORRECTIVE SYSTEM DESIGN: A real-time safety dashboard integrating: (a) live fixed detector readings; (b) active permits with location mapping; (c) automatic permit HOLD if any detector within 30 metres shows H2S > 10 ppm; (d) mandatory acknowledgment of gas history before permit issuance; (e) supervisor cannot override the hold without documented, counter-signed justification.

17.text:
[SOURCE: GENERAL SAFE WORK - Confined Space Entry Gas Testing Requirements]
SAFE WORK PRINCIPLES — CONFINED SPACE ENTRY
Gas Testing Requirements: O2, LEL, H2S, CO

A confined space is defined as any enclosed or partially enclosed space not designed for continuous human occupancy, which has limited means of entry or exit, and which has the potential for hazardous atmosphere or other physical hazards. In industrial facilities, confined spaces include: vessels, tanks, columns, heat exchangers (shell side), sewers, sumps, pits, trenches deeper than 1.5 metres, tunnels, and any enclosed processing equipment.

MANDATORY PRE-ENTRY GAS TESTING: Before any person enters a confined space, gas testing shall confirm ALL of the following conditions are met simultaneously:

(a) OXYGEN: 19.5% to 23.5% v/v. Entry is prohibited if O2 < 19.5% (oxygen deficiency) or > 23.5% (oxygen enrichment — increased combustion risk). Entry into oxygen-deficient atmosphere without SCBA is strictly prohibited.

(b) LEL (Lower Explosive Limit): < 10% LEL. Entry is prohibited if LEL ≥ 10%. Hot work in a confined space requires LEL < 1%.

(c) H2S: < 1 ppm for unprotected entry. Respiratory protection (air-purifying) is required if H2S is between 1-10 ppm. SCBA is mandatory for H2S above 10 ppm. Entry is prohibited without SCBA if H2S ≥ 10 ppm. Do NOT enter if H2S ≥ 50 ppm under any circumstances without emergency rescue equipment and team standby.

(d) CO: < 25 ppm for unprotected entry. Air-purifying respirator for CO 25-50 ppm. SCBA mandatory above 50 ppm. Entry prohibited above 200 ppm without full SCBA and entry attendant.

GAS TESTING LOCATIONS: Testing shall be conducted at three vertical levels — top, middle, and bottom of the confined space — as gas stratification is common. Testing shall also be conducted in any sub-compartments, pockets, or dead-ends within the confined space.

CONTINUOUS MONITORING: During occupied confined space entry, a continuous gas monitor shall be worn by the entrant, with audible and visual alarm. An entry attendant shall remain outside at all times during confined space occupancy.

18.text:
[SOURCE: GENERAL SAFE WORK - Emergency Evacuation Distances for H2S Release]
SAFE WORK PRINCIPLES — EMERGENCY RESPONSE
Emergency Evacuation Distances for H2S Release Scenarios

H2S (Hydrogen Sulfide) is an extremely toxic gas with specific characteristics that govern emergency response distances: it is heavier than air (vapor density 1.19), it can cause rapid incapacitation at IDLH (10 ppm based on NIOSH; 50 ppm based on OSHA in some references), and it causes olfactory fatigue (loss of sense of smell) within minutes of exposure at moderate concentrations, making self-warning unreliable.

EVACUATION DISTANCE MATRIX:

Scenario 1 — MINOR RELEASE (small flange leak, < 1 kg/min estimated rate):
  H2S ≤ 35 ppm at point of detection: Immediate downwind evacuation, 100 metres minimum.
  Action: Isolate source, notify supervisor. Non-essential personnel evacuate.

Scenario 2 — MODERATE RELEASE (pipe failure, 1-10 kg/min estimated rate):
  H2S 35-100 ppm at point of detection: Evacuate 300 metres downwind, 100 metres crosswind.
  Action: Site emergency alarm. Full area evacuation. Emergency services notification.

Scenario 3 — MAJOR RELEASE (catastrophic failure, > 10 kg/min):
  H2S > 100 ppm or detection at multiple monitor locations: Evacuate 500 metres downwind.
  Action: Emergency services called. Incident Commander assumes control. No re-entry without SCBA and incident command authorization.

WIND DIRECTION: All H2S evacuations shall be PERPENDICULAR TO WIND (crosswind) first to move out of the gas plume, then UPWIND when safe distance from source is achieved. NEVER evacuate downwind of a major H2S release.

ASSEMBLY/MUSTER POINTS: Muster points shall be located at minimum 200 metres from the nearest H2S hazard source in the predominant upwind direction, or at a greater distance as determined by site-specific dispersion modeling.

RE-ENTRY CRITERIA: Area shall not be re-entered for normal work until H2S readings at all fixed monitors in the zone have returned to < 1 ppm and remained stable for minimum 30 minutes.

20.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §3.2 Portable Gas Detector Management]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 3.2 — Portable Gas Detector Management

Portable gas detectors are essential instruments for on-the-spot assessment of gas hazards in work areas and confined spaces. This section covers the management, calibration, issue, and use of portable gas detection equipment.

3.2.1 TYPES AND CAPABILITY: All portable gas detectors issued to workers in petroleum or coke oven facilities shall be multi-gas instruments capable of simultaneously measuring at minimum: O2 (electrochemical), LEL (catalytic bead), H2S (electrochemical), and CO (electrochemical). Single-gas instruments are not acceptable as primary protection for hazardous area entry.

3.2.2 BUMP TESTING: Every portable gas detector shall undergo a bump test (functional gas challenge test) before each daily issue or before first use of the day. A bump test record shall be maintained. Any detector failing bump test shall be withdrawn from service immediately and tagged for repair/recalibration.

3.2.3 CALIBRATION: Full calibration of portable detectors shall be conducted: (a) after any failed bump test; (b) after any incident or sensor exposure to high concentration gas; (c) at intervals not exceeding 6 months regardless of bump test results; (d) whenever sensors are replaced.

3.2.4 ALARM HANDLING: Workers shall not silence or disable gas detector alarms in the field. If an alarm activates: (a) stop work immediately; (b) move to fresh air upwind; (c) communicate alarm details (gas type, reading, location) to supervisor; (d) do not re-enter the area until cleared by authorized person. Disabling or silencing alarms without appropriate authority is a disciplinary offense.

3.2.5 ACCOUNTABILITY: A detector issue register shall be maintained. Each detector issued shall be recorded with the worker's name, detector serial number, and bump test result. Return of detectors at end of shift shall be confirmed. Missing detectors shall be reported immediately.

21.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §7.1 Training and Competency for Gas Monitoring Personnel]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 7.1 — Training and Competency Requirements for Gas Monitoring Personnel

7.1.1 All personnel who conduct gas testing for permit-to-work purposes shall hold a valid Gas Tester Competency Certificate issued by the facility's accredited training provider or by a recognized external body. The competency assessment shall include both written examination and practical demonstration.

7.1.2 COMPETENCY ELEMENTS: Gas testers shall demonstrate competency in: (a) identification of all relevant gas hazards in their area; (b) selection and proper use of the appropriate gas detection instrument; (c) conducting pre-use bump test and interpreting results; (d) proper technique for multi-point and multi-level sampling; (e) interpretation of gas readings in the context of permit requirements; (f) actions to take upon alarm condition; (g) documentation and communication of gas test results.

7.1.3 REFRESHER TRAINING: Competency certificates are valid for a maximum of two years. Refresher training and reassessment shall be completed before expiry. Personnel who have been absent from gas testing duties for more than 6 months shall undergo reassessment before being allowed to conduct independent gas testing.

7.1.4 SUPERVISOR TRAINING: All shift supervisors with permit authority shall complete a Gas Hazard Awareness training module covering: IDLH concentrations for all relevant gases; alarm response protocols; SIMOPS implications of gas readings; and their responsibility to review gas monitoring data before issuing permits.

7.1.5 RECORD KEEPING: Training records, certificate copies, and assessment results shall be maintained in personnel files and be available for audit. A competency matrix showing current certification status of all gas testers and permit authorities shall be maintained and reviewed quarterly.

22.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §8.2 Gas Detection in Coke Oven Battery Operations]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 8.2 — Specific Requirements: Gas Detection in Coke Oven Battery Operations

Coke oven battery operations present a unique gas hazard profile due to the co-presence of multiple toxic and flammable gases, high ambient temperatures, and complex process geometry. This section supplements the general requirements of OISD-GS-1 with coke oven-specific provisions.

8.2.1 H2S IN COKE OVEN GAS: Coke oven gas (COG) typically contains H2S at concentrations of 500-2000 mg/Nm³ prior to gas cleaning. Following desulfurization, residual H2S in clean COG should be < 50 mg/Nm³. However, process upsets, desulfurizer overload, or equipment failure can result in breakthrough of high H2S concentrations into the distribution network and battery areas.

8.2.2 FIXED DETECTOR REQUIREMENTS — COKE OVEN SPECIFIC: In addition to general requirements, coke oven battery areas shall have: (a) H2S detectors at every oven door inspection point (battery top), spaced not more than every 5 ovens; (b) multi-gas detector at each collecting main sampling point; (c) H2S detector at each by-product plant inlet and outlet; (d) CO detector at all coke side working platforms.

8.2.3 ELEVATED TEMPERATURE EFFECT: Ambient temperatures in active battery areas can reach 50-70°C, which can affect sensor response times and readings. All fixed detectors in coke oven areas shall be temperature-compensated models rated for operation in the applicable temperature range.

8.2.4 CORRELATION OF GAS READINGS WITH PROCESS EVENTS: A gas monitoring log for coke oven areas shall note the following alongside gas readings: current battery coking cycle position; number of ovens being pushed; status of gas collecting dampers; desulfurizer performance indicators. This contextual data is essential for distinguishing routine process-related gas excursions from developing emergency conditions.

23.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §4.3 Gas Monitoring System Alarms and Control Room Integration]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 4.3 — Gas Monitoring System Alarms and Control Room Integration

4.3.1 CONTROL ROOM DISPLAY: All fixed gas detector readings shall be displayed in real-time on the area control room console. The display shall show: current reading with unit (ppm, % LEL, % v/v); alarm status (normal, low alarm, high alarm, fault); detector identity and location; time of last confirmed calibration.

4.3.2 ALARM ANNUNCIATION: Gas alarms shall be annunciated in the control room with: (a) audio alarm of distinct tone, different from process alarms; (b) visual indication on the mimic diagram showing alarm location; (c) alarm message in the alarm log with timestamp; (d) automatic notification to the shift supervisor's radio/communication device. Alarm management shall comply with IEC 62682 to prevent alarm flooding.

4.3.3 SUPPRESSION AND BYPASS: Gas alarm suppression or bypass shall only be permitted during planned maintenance, calibration, or testing of the detector. Suppression shall: (a) require authorization from the Shift Supervisor; (b) be time-limited (maximum 4 hours unless extended with re-authorization); (c) require compensatory gas monitoring by portable detector at the suppressed detector location at minimum every 30 minutes; (d) be logged in the alarm management system with reason and expected reinstatement time. Any suppressed detector that is not reinstated within 24 hours shall be escalated to the site Safety Officer.

4.3.4 DATA RETENTION: Control system gas monitoring data shall be retained for minimum 90 days in online storage and 5 years in archive. Historical data shall be retrievable in tabular or graphical format for incident investigation purposes.

25.text:
[SOURCE: VIZAG INCIDENT ANALYSIS - Shift Handover Gas Briefing Failure Pattern]
VIZAG STEEL PLANT SAFETY ANALYSIS — SHIFT HANDOVER FAILURE ANALYSIS
Subject: Elevated Gas Reading Communication Failures at Shift Change

ANALYSIS OF SHIFT HANDOVER FAILURES IN FATAL INCIDENTS:

In three of five fatal incidents investigated, the critical elevated gas readings (H2S > 20 ppm) that preceded the fatal event were present during the outgoing shift but were not effectively communicated to the incoming shift supervisor. This analysis examines the failure modes:

FAILURE MODE A — NORMALIZATION OF DEVIATION: Outgoing supervisors had observed elevated H2S readings on multiple prior shifts and had come to regard readings in the 20-35 ppm range as "normal for this area." This normalization meant elevated readings were not highlighted as priority items during handover. The incoming supervisors received the information but did not treat it as requiring urgent action.

FAILURE MODE B — INCOMPLETE HANDOVER DOCUMENTATION: Handover documentation was completed in paper logs where gas readings were recorded in a general remarks section. There was no mandatory field specifically for "elevated gas readings" or "Priority-1 safety items." The elevated readings were present in the log but not visually distinguished from routine comments.

FAILURE MODE C — TIME PRESSURE ON HANDOVER: Shift handovers occurred during meal breaks when both supervisors were expected to complete the handover and take their meal in a combined 30-minute window. Abbreviated handovers resulted in key safety information being communicated incompletely.

RECOMMENDATIONS: (1) Implement a mandatory color-coded Priority-1 Gas Alert flag in handover documentation that requires explicit acknowledgment by incoming supervisor; (2) Ensure shift handover has protected, uninterrupted time allocation (minimum 20 minutes); (3) Any H2S reading > 15 ppm during a shift should automatically trigger a "Gas Status Elevated" notification that remains visible in the control room until explicitly cleared by the incoming supervisor.

26.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §9.1 Emergency Response Planning for Gas Releases]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 9.1 — Emergency Response Planning for Gas Releases

9.1.1 SITE EMERGENCY PLAN: Every facility covered by this standard shall maintain a current Site Emergency Plan (SEP) that specifically addresses gas release scenarios. The SEP shall be reviewed annually and updated within 30 days of any significant incident or change in plant layout.

9.1.2 GAS RELEASE SCENARIOS: The SEP shall include documented response plans for: (a) minor toxic gas release — H2S < 10 ppm at point of release, confined to equipment; (b) moderate toxic gas release — H2S 10-100 ppm, potential personnel exposure; (c) major toxic gas release — H2S > 100 ppm, multiple detectors activating, large area affected; (d) coke oven gas release (combined H2S, CO, and flammable hazard); (e) confined space rescue — person incapacitated in confined space with toxic atmosphere.

9.1.3 EMERGENCY RESPONSE TEAM (ERT): An ERT shall be on standby during all operational shifts. ERT members shall be trained in: gas rescue operations, SCBA use and rescue harness use, victim decontamination procedures, first aid for H2S exposure (including positioning of unconscious victim and administration of oxygen). ERT response time from alarm to team deployment shall not exceed 5 minutes.

9.1.4 FIRST AID FOR H2S EXPOSURE: Personnel exposed to H2S shall receive immediate first aid: (a) remove victim from exposure zone — ONLY using SCBA-equipped rescuers; (b) move to fresh air; (c) administer 100% supplemental oxygen; (d) if victim is not breathing, initiate CPR; (e) call ambulance immediately; (f) all exposed persons shall receive medical evaluation within 1 hour of exposure, even if asymptomatic. H2S can cause delayed pulmonary effects.

9.1.5 DRILL FREQUENCY: Gas release emergency drills shall be conducted at minimum quarterly. At least one annual drill shall simulate a major H2S release scenario with full ERT activation and muster point accountability.

27.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-2 §3.3 Flare System Requirements During Emergency Blowdown]
OISD Standard GS-2: Blow Down and Pressure Relief Systems
Section 3.3 — Flare System Requirements During Emergency Blowdown

3.3.1 FLARE SYSTEM AVAILABILITY: The flare system shall be maintained in operational condition at all times when the plant is processing. An automated flare tip ignition system (pilot ignition) shall be maintained with automatic reignition on pilot flame failure.

3.3.2 H2S IN BLOWDOWN STREAMS: In facilities processing sour crude, sour gas, or operating coke oven gas systems, blowdown and pressure relief streams will contain H2S. The flare system shall be designed to ensure complete combustion of H2S (converting to SO2) under all expected blowdown flow conditions. Incomplete combustion of H2S-containing streams constitutes a toxic gas release emergency.

3.3.3 MONITORING DURING BLOWDOWN: During any emergency blowdown: (a) continuous H2S monitoring shall be maintained at the flare base area — minimum 3 detectors at 120° spacing around the flare stack; (b) SO2 monitoring shall be maintained downwind of the flare; (c) all non-essential personnel shall be evacuated from within 200 metres of the flare stack during major blowdown events.

3.3.4 WIND-ASSISTED DISPERSION: Emergency management during major blowdown events shall account for prevailing wind direction. Muster points and evacuation routes shall be adjusted based on current wind conditions to ensure personnel are not directed into the path of potential flare gas dispersion.

28.text:
[SOURCE: FACTORY ACT 1948 Section 41C - Right of Workers to Warn About Dangers]
THE FACTORIES ACT 1948 (as amended)
Chapter IV-A: Special Provisions Relating to Hazardous Processes
Section 41C — Right of Workers to Warn and Take Protective Action

41C(1) Without prejudice to any other provisions in this Act, every worker employed in a hazardous process shall have the right — and indeed the duty — to warn the management of any condition that the worker reasonably believes to be hazardous to life, health, or safety.

41C(2) Any worker who, on reasonable grounds, believes that continuing to work in the prevailing conditions will expose them or other workers to serious bodily harm, shall have the right to withdraw from such area or operation without being subject to any disciplinary action, provided that: (a) the worker notifies their supervisor of the hazard before withdrawal where this is reasonably practicable; (b) the withdrawal is in good faith.

41C(3) No occupier or manager shall: (a) dismiss or threaten dismissal of a worker who raises a safety concern or exercises withdrawal rights under this section; (b) penalize, demote, or otherwise adversely affect the employment of such a worker; (c) take any coercive action to compel the worker to return to work before the hazard is adequately addressed.

41C(4) SPECIFIC APPLICATION TO GAS HAZARDS: Any worker who detects, through portable or fixed gas monitoring equipment, gas concentrations at or above IDLH levels has the right to refuse to enter or continue work in the affected area. This right is absolute and cannot be overridden by production priorities or supervisor instructions. The worker shall report the detection immediately through normal communication channels to trigger the appropriate emergency response.

APPLICATION NOTE: This provision reinforces OISD-GS-1 requirements for alarm response by providing legal backing for workers who stop work and evacuate upon gas alarm activation.

29.text:
[SOURCE: GENERAL SAFE WORK - Combined Toxic Load and Synergistic Effects]
SAFE WORK PRINCIPLES — MULTI-GAS HAZARD ASSESSMENT
Combined Toxic Load and Synergistic Effects in Coke Oven and Petroleum Environments

In industrial environments where multiple toxic gases may be present simultaneously, the hazard is frequently greater than the sum of individual gas risks. This section addresses the concept of combined toxic load and synergistic effects.

COMBINED TOXICITY IN COKE OVEN GAS (COG):
Coke oven gas typically contains: H2S (toxic, IDLH 50 ppm); CO (toxic, IDLH 1200 ppm); Benzene (carcinogenic, IDLH 500 ppm); Naphthalene; Ammonia; Light hydrocarbons (flammable). When personnel are exposed to COG, the combined toxic burden means that individual gas readings well below individual IDLH values can still produce significant health effects.

COMBINED RISK ASSESSMENT RULE: Apply the Additive Toxicity Index (ATI) where multiple gases are known to affect the same target organ or system. For H2S and CO (both affecting cellular oxygen utilization):
ATI = (Observed H2S ppm / IDLH H2S) + (Observed CO ppm / IDLH CO)
If ATI > 0.5: Treat as Level-2 Caution condition.
If ATI > 1.0: Treat as IDLH condition — full emergency response.

Example: H2S = 15 ppm, CO = 150 ppm.
ATI = (15/50) + (150/1200) = 0.30 + 0.125 = 0.425 — approaching caution threshold.

Example: H2S = 25 ppm, CO = 200 ppm.
ATI = (25/50) + (200/1200) = 0.50 + 0.167 = 0.667 — Level-2 Caution threshold exceeded.

PRACTICAL GUIDANCE: In coke oven battery areas and gas cleaning plants, never evaluate H2S alone. Always assess alongside CO and check ATI. A reading of H2S 25 ppm alongside CO 200 ppm in a location where confined space entry is planned constitutes a CRITICAL combined hazard regardless of individual alarm thresholds not being exceeded.

30.text:
[SOURCE: OISD-GS-1 §6.1 Confined Space Entry Permit Coordination with Gas Detection]
OISD Standard GS-1: Gas Detection and Alarm Systems in Petroleum Industry
Section 6.1 — Confined Space Entry Permit Coordination with Gas Detection

6.1.1 The confined space entry permit and the gas monitoring data are inseparable documents. No confined space entry permit shall be considered valid without current gas test results attached that meet the conditions specified in this section and in Section 3.1.3.

6.1.2 CONTINUOUS MONITORING MANDATORY: Unlike some permit activities where periodic gas testing may be acceptable, all occupied confined space entries shall have continuous monitoring. The entrant shall carry a personal multi-gas detector with audible alarm. An external gas monitor shall simultaneously monitor at the confined space opening.

6.1.3 RESCUE PLAN BEFORE ENTRY: Before any person enters a confined space, a rescue plan shall be documented and the rescue equipment shall be in place at the confined space entry point. This shall include: a rescue harness on the entrant, a lifeline attended by the entry attendant, SCBA available for rescuers. NO entry is permitted without a documented rescue plan.

6.1.4 ISOLATION FROM ADJACENT OPERATIONS: When a person is inside a confined space, the following operations shall not be permitted within 15 metres: hot work of any kind; pressurized liquid or gas line connections or disconnections; operations that could generate additional gas hazards.

6.1.5 COMMUNICATION: The entrant and the entry attendant shall maintain continuous communication — verbal, visual, or via rope signal. Communication shall be attempted at minimum every 5 minutes. If the attendant loses communication with the entrant, the confined space entry shall be treated as an emergency and rescue procedures initiated immediately.

6.1.6 ALARM RESPONSE INSIDE CONFINED SPACE: If any gas alarm activates — whether from the entrant's personal detector or from the external monitor — the entrant shall evacuate immediately via the established egress route. No investigation, troubleshooting, or work completion shall be attempted. Evacuation takes priority over all other actions.
